ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for tandoori-marinated chicken; A0CKM (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of tandoori chicken is approximately 2 pieces (roughly 250 g), equivalent to half a spring chicken, as commonly served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Chicken — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), Table: Poultry and Egg Products
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) includes a direct entry for tandoori chicken (roasted, with skin removed). Values per 100 g edible portion are used as reported. Minor micronutrient fields not reported in the source are set to -1 per sentinel convention.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of High-Protein, Low-Carbohydrate Foods — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008). International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 30 based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked poultry dishes. Protein-dominant foods with minimal starch consistently yield GI values in the 20–35 range per Atkinson et al. (2008) and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 6.25) / 100 = 1.9, where serving carbohydrate = 2.5 g/100g × 250 g serving = 6.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) from marinade spices and salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid additional salt or high-sodium chutneys, as the marinade contributes approximately 800 mg sodium per 250 g serving, representing roughly 35% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~2.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tandoori chicken is a suitable protein-rich, low-GI option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a 250 g serving contributes only approximately 6 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~1.9, producing minimal postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (5.5 g/100 g) with relatively low saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) when skin is removed
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori chicken prepared without skin is a lean, high-protein poultry option with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request skin-removed preparation and pair with vegetables rather than high-fat accompaniments such as butter naan or cream-based sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori chicken is a classic North Indian dish in which whole chicken or chicken pieces are marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture — typically containing red chilli powder, turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and salt — and then cooked at very high temperatures in a traditional clay tandoor oven. The result is a dish with a characteristic smoky, charred exterior, vibrant red-orange colour from the chilli and spices, and a juicy, tender interior. The yoghurt marinade tenderises the meat and imparts a mild tang, while the high-heat cooking seals in moisture. Tandoori chicken is a staple of Punjabi cuisine and is widely served across Indian restaurants in Singapore, often as a starter or as part of a mixed grill platter. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 25 g protein and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it one of the most nutritionally favourable Indian restaurant dishes for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following a high-protein diet. Its glycemic index is very low (estimated ~30), and a standard 250 g serving contributes a glycemic load of under 2. Sodium content is moderate (~320 mg/100 g) due to the spiced marinade, which warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. When prepared without skin, the saturated fat content is low (~1.5 g/100 g), supporting cardiovascular health. In Singapore, tandoori chicken is commonly served in portions of 2, 4, or 8 pieces, accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori chicken?
Is tandoori chicken good for diabetics?
Is tandoori chicken high in protein?
Can I eat tandoori chicken if I have high blood pressure?
What is tandoori chicken made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), which includes a direct entry for tandoori chicken; this is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed tables for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked poultry, as no direct GI measurement for tandoori chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Serving size is set to 250 g (approximately 2 pieces of spring chicken), representing the smallest listed portion option (2 pieces) from the field survey listing. Larger portions (4 pieces ~500 g, 8 pieces ~1000 g) are multiples of this base serving. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food entry. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food entry. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food entry. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN; naturally negligible in lean poultry prepared without hydrogenated fats). 6. GI is an analogous estimate of 30 based on the high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked poultry category per Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for tandoori chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The food_name has been normalised to the canonical form 'Tandoori Chicken', removing the portion-size descriptor from the field survey listing title. 8. Halal status is flagged as true as tandoori chicken is traditionally prepared with halal chicken at Indian Muslim and North Indian restaurants in Singapore; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. 9. Gluten-free flag is true as the standard marinade contains no wheat-based ingredients; consumers with coeliac disease should verify cross-contamination risk with the restaurant.
